title: Spin Dynamics from Niu-Kleinman Adiabatic Approach and Slave Boson Mean Field Theory
authors: Xuan Yang, Tianyang Xie, Shaohang Shi, Kun Jiang, Jiangping Hu
abstract: Spin-wave excitations provide a central probe of magnetic order and electronic correlations in strongly correlated materials. In this work, we develop an adiabatic theory of spin dynamics by combining the Niu-Kleinman formalism with Kotliar-Ruckenstein slave-boson theory (NK+KRSB). For each frozen spin configuration, the constrained slave-boson saddle point is solved self-consistently, allowing the Berry-curvature matrix and energy Hessian entering the linearized adiabatic equations of motion to be extracted directly. Applied to the half-filled single-orbital Hubbard model, the resulting spin-wave dispersion shows substantially improved agreement with determinant quantum Monte Carlo benchmarks compared with the random phase approximation and closely approaches results from the time-dependent Gutzwiller approximation. We further extend the method to a two-orbital model of $\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{NiO}_4$, demonstrating its applicability to realistic multi-orbital correlated systems. Because the approach only requires saddle-point solutions near the magnetic ground state, it remains computationally efficient while incorporating strong-correlation effects beyond conventional weak-coupling descriptions, providing a practical framework for studying low-energy spin excitations in correlated quantum materials.
summary: This paper introduces a novel theoretical framework combining the Niu-Kleinman adiabatic theory with slave-boson mean-field theory to study spin dynamics in correlated materials. By solving the constrained slave-boson saddle point self-consistently, the authors derive spin-wave dispersions for the Hubbard model and $\text{La}_2\text{NiO}_4$. This method successfully incorporates strong correlation effects while remaining computationally tractable for studying low-energy magnetic excitations.
main_problem: Developing an efficient theoretical framework to study low-energy spin excitations (spin dynamics) in strongly correlated quantum materials.
main_result: The combined Niu-Kleinman adiabatic theory with slave-boson mean-field theory yields spin-wave dispersions that show substantially improved agreement with Quantum Monte Carlo benchmarks compared to simpler approximations like RPA.
method: The approach combines the Niu-Kleinman adiabatic formalism with the Kotliar-Ruckenstein slave-boson mean-field theory (NK+KRSB) to derive the spin dynamics equation of motion.
model_or_system: The method is applied to the single-orbital Hubbard model and extended to a two-orbital model relevant to $\text{La}_2\text{NiO}_4$, allowing study of realistic multi-orbital correlated systems.
key_observables: Spin-wave dispersion ($\omega(\mathbf{q})$) and the Berry-curvature matrix.
important_parameters: Half-filled filling, strong interaction $U$, and the lattice structure (square lattice for the Hubbard model).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is the adiabatic separation of low-energy spin dynamics from higher-energy charge/orbital excitations, and the use of saddle-point approximations for the slave bosons.
figures_summary: Figure 2 compares the calculated spin-wave dispersion against benchmarks; Figure 3 shows the calculated dispersion for $\text{La}_2\text{NiO}_4$ compared to inelastic neutron scattering data.
paper_structure: The paper reviews the NK formalism, develops the method for the single-orbital Hubbard model, extends it to multi-orbital systems ($\text{La}_2\text{NiO}_4$), and concludes by demonstrating the method's utility.
why_interesting: The methodology provides a powerful, computationally efficient way to calculate low-energy excitations in strongly correlated systems, bridging the gap between simple mean-field theories and highly accurate but complex benchmarks like DQMC.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Workflow for computing the spin wave energy at a fixed spiral wavevector ⃗q. The central step is to obtain a family of saddle point solutions for the same ⃗q, including the reference state and nearby perturbed states, from which the Berry curvature matrix and the energy Hessian are constructed.

FIG. 2: Spin wave dispersion of the half filled single orbital Hubbard model obtained from different methods. The parameters are U = 8.0 and n = 1.0. The method developed in this work is denoted by NK+KRSB, and the calculation is performed on 80 × 80 magnetic unit cells. The triangle markers show DQMC results on an 8 × 8 lattice, which has no sign problem at half filling. NK+KRSB, similar to TDGA, agrees better with the DQMC results than RPA does, especially around ⃗q = (π, 0).

FIG. 3: Spin wave dispersion of La2NiO4. In the calculation, the interaction parameters are chosen as U = 6.0 eV and JH = 1.8 eV, and the lattice has 64 × 64 magnetic unit cells. The gap in the experimental spectrum originates from spin orbit coupling, which is not included in the present calculation.

FIG. 4: Comparing the result of RPA and NK+HF method. U = 8.0. The RPA result is almost the same as NK+HF, which indicates that the Niu-Kleinman adiabatic equation captures the essential physics in RPA, at least in the low energy regime.
